living-concrete.knowledge: TYPE: How to execute tasks -- step-by-step instructions. EXAMPLES: Coding syntax, clinical techniques, recipes, language rules. DIGESTION: Immediate real-world practice. Cannot be learned by reading. PRINCIPLE: Practice must follow consumption promptly -- delayed practice = retention failure. MECHANISM: Embeds skills through motor/procedural repetition, develops automaticity.

TYPE: Knowledge connecting new concepts to existing understanding via comparison. EXAMPLES: "Circuits are like water pipes"; muscle physiology related to swimming. DIGESTION: Systematic critique -- analyze similarities, differences, limitations, improvements. PRINCIPLE: Refuse surface-level acceptance. Uncritical analogy = shallow encoding + misconceptions. KEY QUESTIONS: Where does it hold? Where does it break? What would make it better? MECHANISM: Builds relational thinking by forcing structural engagement with comparisons.

TYPE: High-level theories, frameworks, interconnected ideas -- the "big picture." EXAMPLES: Disease mechanisms, mathematical relationships, evolutionary biology. DIGESTION: Nonlinear visual mapping -- mind maps, diagrams showing hierarchies and cause-effect. PRINCIPLE: Visualize how disparate facts interconnect within broader intellectual structures. HIGHEST VALUE: Forms the structural skeleton that other types attach to. CRITICAL: Linear notes preserve sequence of presentation, not structure of understanding. Mapping forces identification of relationships: cause, contain, contradict.

TYPE: Empirical facts, statistics, dates, specific cases supporting broader concepts. EXAMPLES: Research statistics, historical events, case study data. DIGESTION: Two-step -- store during consumption (notes), then rehearse (teach, discuss, apply). PRINCIPLE: Evidence supports conceptual arguments; useless without the concept it attaches to. MECHANISM: Rehearsal forces integration of evidence with its conceptual context.

TYPE: Supplementary specific details -- formulas, constants, definitions, lookup data. EXAMPLES: Mathematical constants, API parameter formats, gene names. DIGESTION: Store in searchable systems; review periodically via spaced repetition (~30 min daily). LOWEST PRIORITY: Minimal processing effort. Strategic offload -- not everything needs deep encoding. PRINCIPLE: Brain's processing capacity is finite; spending it on reference crowds out higher-value work on conceptual and analogous information.

Learning has two stages. Most learners only do stage 1.
STAGE 1 - CONSUMPTION: Intake of information. Identify PACER category in real-time. Necessary but insufficient. What goes into the brain.
STAGE 2 - DIGESTION: Processing via type-specific technique into long-term memory. What stays in the brain. The stage that produces actual learning.
THE BALANCE: Everything consumed MUST be digested. Overconsumption without digestion = "mental vomiting" -- information passes through without retention. ~90% forgetting rate for unprocessed material.
PRACTICAL: If you cannot digest, consume LESS. Speed and volume are counterproductive without corresponding digestion time.

P, A, C = foundational knowledge. E, R = supporting.
COMMON ERROR: Excessive time on procedural memorization and reference details instead of building conceptual foundation. Without conceptual structure, evidence is disconnected facts and references are meaningless details.
INVESTMENT ORDER (processing effort): 1. Conceptual -- highest ROI, structural skeleton 2. Analogous -- relational anchoring to existing knowledge 3. Procedural -- skill execution (cannot substitute reading) 4. Evidence -- supports concepts, rehearsal-based 5. Reference -- strategic offload, minimal processing

PACER synthesizes established cognitive science:
Craik & Lockhart (1972) - Levels of Processing: Deeper semantic processing = longer retention. PACER digestion strategies force depth appropriate to each type.
Schema Theory: Knowledge organized in mental schemas. Conceptual mapping directly builds and extends schemas.
Sweller (1988) - Cognitive Load Theory: Working memory is limited. PACER priority hierarchy (C > E > R) manages cognitive load by directing effort to most productive categories.
Ebbinghaus (1885) - Spaced Repetition: Memory decays but reinforces through spaced review. PACER assigns spaced repetition specifically to E and R categories.
Bloom's Taxonomy (revised 2001): Higher-order skills as entry points, not endpoints. Analysis produces memorization as byproduct.

Natural convergences between PACER and BentOS architecture:
| PACER | BentOS | Mapping | | Consumption + Digestion | Wake + Sleep | Intake then consolidate | | Balance principle | Signal discipline | Don't intake more than you consolidate | | Organic rewrite | Higher-order encoding | Restructure understanding, don't append | | task-tracker-mem | Lower-order encoding | Recording events without encoding meaning | | Kim Peak paradox | task-tracker-mem | Perfect recall, zero reasoning | | Priority hierarchy (C > R) | Signal discipline | Invest in structure, offload details | | Encoding quality > quantity | Token economics | Process less, consolidate better |
Anamnesis sleep IS digestion. Plasticity IS self-regulated learning. These convergences were discovered independently and confirmed by R1.0.

PACER is bidirectional. Designed as receiver-side (learning), it inverts to sender-side (content engineering).
The same cognitive science that describes how information is best RECEIVED also prescribes how information should be TRANSMITTED.
Receiver: classify incoming info, apply type-specific digestion. Sender: deliberately structure output so reader hits P-A-C-E-R modes in the right sequence and depth.
This inversion is the bridge to craft/structuring/content-direction.
living-concrete.pattern: 1. BEGIN CONSUMPTION -- read/listen/observe, classify each piece by PACER type 2. PAUSE FOR DIGESTION at natural breakpoints: P: stop and practice immediately A: critique the analogy (hold? break? improve?) C: map the relationships (diagram, mind map, network) E: store in notes, schedule rehearsal (teach, discuss) R: store in flashcard/searchable system, add to spaced repetition 3. MAINTAIN BALANCE -- if digestion backs up, reduce consumption rate 4. REPEAT -- each cycle deepens understanding
living-concrete.antipattern: DON'T: Apply the same strategy to all information types. DO: Classify first, then apply type-specific digestion. Reading a concept the same way you store a reference = wasted processing.
DON'T: Speed-read, binge content, consume without digesting. DO: Consume less, digest more. Balance is non-negotiable. "Mental vomiting" -- information passes through without being retained.
